sentences.text: He remained head of the Documentation Division, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I believe, up until January of 1946.
But meanwhile, just before the trial begin-- began in November, maybe three weeks beforehand, Justice Jackson made him the chief of a kind of a review group of top officers.
I was not on the group, and-- but we reported to that group now and then.
And-- and Jackson liked the way Storey handled people so well that, all of a sudden, he was announced to be executive trial counsel, and that was before the trial started.

And Storey actually had-- after Justice Jackson made his opening statement, his famous opening statement on November 21, 1945, Storey took over and indicated the manner in which we hoped to present documents.
It turned out to have a lot of flaws in it, but-- because we didn't have enough translations, and enough translations into French and into Russian, particularly.
And this caused a great consternation.
And the tribunal stood up and said through Justice-- through President Lawrence, I don't want any documents put in here which are not in the language of all three of the other languages than German.
So what happened was that we had to read the documents we were putting into evidence, and they would be interpreted simultaneously into French and into Russian and English.
And if the document happened to be a French document, why, it'd be the-- into the other three languages, of course.
It had to be translated.
Now, if it was translated, if a document was translated into the-- all the working languages of the court, you could introduce the document, assuming it was relevant.
But if it wasn't, you had to read from the document, and that slowed the proceedings down.

We hadn't figured out how that was going to work until the tribunal confronted the prosecution with the point that, look, you got to slow this trial down to be sure the defense and their attorneys can keep up with it immediately.
So this led to a tremendous amount of additional translating and paperwork.
Peter Eberhaal flew to England and with the tremendous priority attached to his work managed to recruit a large-- a larger number of translators and interpreters.
And he was an expert linguist himself in three languages-- German, French, and English-- and he did a magnificent job.
Well, then at that time, Lieutenant Eberhaal was-- he was born and raised in Vienna.

And when Hitler came to power, he chose to leave immediately and somehow got to the United States and worked his way up until he was first a-- a lieutenant, and he later went on and became a lieutenant colonel.
He had a-- stayed in the-- in the Army for a long time.
He went to Vietnam.
He went to a lot of other places, partly because of his linguistic skills.
Well, they were-- they were a great variety of people.
Some of them came from Switzerland.
Some of them came from Austria.

Some of them-- a large number of them came from Great Britain, because the number of the refugees who spoke both languages-- both German and English-- had fled into Great Britain, and we recruited them there.
The colonel-- or then Lieutenant Eberhaal, flew to-- to London and actually did a lot of recruiting there with the help of OSS and so on.
There was a great variety.
There were some Dutch people who became interpreters.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A few Polish people, who'd managed to get out one way or another, came back in as interpreters.
And of course, the Czechs, who usually can speak three or four languages, we usually-- we had quite a few Czechs who we recruited and used.
This commander that you saw last night, whose name escapes me right now, who was at the Nuremberg reunion, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was head of the Language Division.
And he had had experiences in languages, and he immediately got hold of some top people.
And then Lieutenant Dostert, D-O-S-T-E-R-T, was the person who helped install the simultaneous translation equipment, simultaneous interpretation equipment, which had been furnished by IBM.
There's a story about that which may interest you.
The initial plan had been to have interpreters who would have to sit and listen to each sentence or phrase spoken and interpret in the other language.
There'd be a pause while this was being done, and then the speaker could go on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This would be-- take a lot of time and, of course, it would have slowed the proceedings down a great deal.

But someone from IBM had been-- had furnished some linguistic mechanisms whereby a speaker could speak into an autophone, and it would be carried to another translator who would then speak the same thing in a different language as closely as he could.
And this would be recorded and-- or available in that language.
And Charlie Horsky, who I think you've interviewed, was back in Washington as the-- one of the early representatives of Justice Jackson, and he heard about this.
And he actually cabled to Justice Jackson that he thought it might be worth trying.
And Jackson immediately said, by all means, work on it.
They did, and Nuremberg became the first place where simultaneous translation was used on a very extensive basis throughout the whole trial.
He-- he had been hesitant because of his war service, which had taken him to many countries.
He'd been hesitant to commit himself to stay with Justice Jackson for any period of time.
And he had, I think, at the [? onset, ?] I would-- I can't possibly stay because of my needs to go back to Texas later than Christmas.
So actually, he stayed until about January 7 or 8 or something like that.
And he actually put in, himself, some of the main evidence on the accused organizations, on the leadership corps, the Reich cabinet, and so forth.
And then he, pursuant to agreement that Jackson had made much earlier, he left and returned to Texas and had a distinguished career there.
Who replaced him at Nuremberg?
He was replaced by Thomas J.
Dodd, who later became a United States senator, and whose son is now a United States senator, Christopher Dodd from Connecticut.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tom Dodd was a-- had been a-- a trial attorney and a prosecutor underneath Justice Jackson when Justice Jackson was attorney general, and he'd had quite a bit of experience with the Department of Justice and otherwise.
So he was a very experienced trial attorney.
He was a part of the Interrogation Division at the beginning, just before the trial began and after it started.
And the work he did so impressed Justice Jackson that he wanted him to be-- to succeed Colonel Storey.
Actually, by the time Dodd was made executive trial counsel, I had become temporary head of a new division called Subsequent Proceedings.
I knew this wouldn't last long, because there would have to be a successor to Jackson, who had high rank, and eventually the Subsequent Proceedings would become the main trial.
And that's, of course, what happened.
Colonel Taylor, Colonel Tulford Taylor, became the designate-- the designated successor to Justice Jackson, and he took over the Subsequent Proceedings.
And I then became head of the Economics Division and later head of the IG Farben trial team and later head of the Publications Division under General Taylor.
General Taylor was promoted-- Colonel Taylor was promoted to general some time in the spring of 1945.
It was very plain from the time we began that people who were, you know, high authority in Nazi Germany reached far beyond the 24 people that we indicted in the first trial.
And Justice Jackson, in his early reports to President Truman, indicated that there would have to be some subsequent trials, but that he was only committed to the first trial.
And very early, he indicated and, in fact, this-- in December of 1945, that he was probably planning on having Colonel Taylor become his successor, or recommending that he be made his successor.
And indeed, this was carried on to where Tulford Taylor was made the deputy chief counsel for Subsequent Proceedings, as well as for the main trial.

And as soon as Justice Jackson resigned in October, just after the first trial was done, General McNarney, the head of the US military forces in Germany, appointed then Colonel Taylor to be US Chief of Counsel for War Crimes.
The International Military Tribunal.
In the Subsequent Proceedings, the tribunals were merely referred to as the American Military Tribunal for Case 1 or Case 2 or whatever it was, and we had 12 cases.
There were four members.

These were Judge Biddle, representing the United States, Lord Geoffrey Lawrence, representing Great Britain, Nikachenko, a general representing the Soviets, and Donadeu Vabres, V-A-B-R-E-S, representing the French.
Each of them had a deputy so that if something happened to them, the deputy could fill in without disturbing the progress of the trial.
So there were really four main judges and four assistant judges.
Well, of course, the prosecution had nothing to do with it.
But as I understand it from the biography of Judge Biddle and by other reports that we have since the trial, they would normally agree on a consensual basis after discussion on points.

And if there was a dissent, what they would do is file a quiet memorandum so that this-- if there was disagreement, let's say three to one, in a vote, it wouldn't-- on a particular point as the trial was going on, it wouldn't necessarily appear on the record.
The tribunal would merely say that the vote of the tribunal-- the decision of the tribunal is such and such.
And if there happened to be a dissent, nothing would be said about it at the time, because you can't have a lot of-- of dissents going on during the course of the trial.
I think that when they came to the votes on guilt or innocence, there were unanimous votes with respect to all the 19 defendants who were convicted.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] With respect to the three who were acquitted, von Papen, Fritzche, and Schacht, there was a dissent by the Soviet judge.

And that dissent was not ready at the conclusion of the trial, so Lord Justice Lawrence merely indicated-- the president of the tribunal-- merely indicated that there would be a dissent filed by the Soviet judge later on.
And it was about three weeks later before we even got a copy of it.
Well, the prosecution was divided into four parts.

But since the United States had taken the initiative through President Truman and Justice Jackson, had really been the main pushers for a trial of the main Nazi war criminals, the Americans, right from the beginning, were assumed to be those who would kind of carry the main burden.
And we immediately added to Jackson's staff the-- a large number of interpreters and a large number of lawyers who were interested in-- in working with him.
And the staff far exceeded in numbers [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that of the British, the French, and the Soviets put together.

The reason that Nuremberg was chosen as the place of the trial was partly because everybody felt that the Americans would-- could pay the expenses of putting this on, but mainly because General Clay, or someone representing him, had recommended that Nuremberg be looked at in view of the fact that there was this huge Palace of Justice, as it had been named underneath the Weimer Republic, the-- and maybe even before, in Nuremberg, which was hardly-- the edifice had not been basically affected, apart from the windows being blown in and one little corner of one part of the building.

And when the Americans said, we are convinced this would make a good place, and Justice Jackson flew there with the French and the British representatives, the Russians said, we aren't too interested.
They wanted to go back to Moscow to have some conferences anyway.
So they never actually had a part in making that decision.
When they came back to the London negotiations, everybody voted for Nuremberg and the Russians said, well, we'll go along with that.
But they did insist on having the headquarters of the tribunal be in Berlin, but that was merely a temporary thing.
The indictment was filed, and the first meeting of the tribunal was held in-- in Berlin.
And then immediately the proceedings were moved to Nuremberg.
Nuremberg was in the American area of occupation.
Yes.

The-- the general idea was that the Americans would carry the-- the conspiracy charge, that the defendants were associated as principals or accessories in the commission of these crimes, and that we would have the task of proving that.
That was suggested by Judge Nikachenko, the Soviet judge, when they were starting to lay out the way the indictment might be formed.

And in addition to the conspiracy charge, there would be a charge of the actual waging and preparing for the war, and then a charge on war crimes, which were more or less the traditional kind of crimes, and then crimes against humanity, which included the crimes of oppression and persecution and the kinds of things which were-- had not previously been tried as crimes before tribunals.
The Americans, given the conspiracy case and having the largest number of staff, actually extended the conspiracy to reach into every part of the case so that by the time-- well, let me back up.

The British, particularly under Sir David Maxwell Fyfe, who was the deputy chief prosecutor-- and he had come from the Tory government, the conservative government, and had actually been the chair of the-- the representatives from the four powers in London when they negotiated the London Agreement and the London Charter.
He was very instrumental in influencing the-- not only the keeping up good relations between the four powers-- he was extremely good at that.
He was an unusual diplomat, a man of exceeding great ability.
He later became Lord Chancellor and was Lord Kilmuir, K-I-L-M-U-I-R.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

By the time the defense case came along, the president of the tribunal, who was British, and Sir David Maxwell Fyfe would often, right out in open court, say, well, Sir David, will you take care of doing this?
Will you go and-- and be sure that the defense gets this document or that?
That if they want some witnesses from other countries, will you help make arrangements for that?
So he became a really tremendous force in the trial, far beyond his rank.
The British had only eight [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] barristers there, and they had several attorneys who were not barristers but never appeared in court.
Only those eight did.
Whereas the Americans had a total of some 27 attorneys who became trial counsel, actual prosecutors who appeared before the tribunal.
And I was one of them, yes.
In January or early February, I presented two cases.
The first case was against Baldur von Schirach, the youth leader and the Gauleiter of Vienna after 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The second one was Hans Fritzche, who was the main Nazi propagandist.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those were the two defendants that I had, and I presented those cases early in 1946.
